Stomach.name: Stomach
Stomach.function: A muscular bag that churns food with acid and enzymes, breaking a meal down into a soupy mix. Carbohydrates start turning into simple sugars here, and the stomach controls how fast that mix is released into the gut — which sets how quickly your blood sugar will rise after eating.

Duodenum.name: Duodenum
Duodenum.function: The first stretch of small intestine, just below the stomach. As food arrives it releases incretin hormones such as GLP-1 that tell the pancreas to get ready with insulin before sugar even hits the blood. It also receives bile and pancreatic enzymes that finish digestion. (GLP-1 is the hormone the new weight-loss and diabetes drugs imitate.)

Pancreas.name: Pancreas
Pancreas.function: The master gland of blood sugar. Tiny clusters called the islets of Langerhans release insulin to lower glucose after a meal and glucagon to raise it when you are low — a constant balancing act. The rest of the pancreas makes digestive enzymes. When its insulin supply fails or the body stops responding, the result is diabetes.

Liver.name: Liver
Liver.function: The body's glucose bank. After a meal it stores excess sugar as glycogen under insulin's command; between meals and overnight it releases glucose back into the blood under glucagon's command to keep you steady. This is why your blood sugar stays stable for hours without eating.

Gallbladder.name: Gallbladder
Gallbladder.function: A small pouch tucked under the liver that stores and concentrates bile. When a fatty meal reaches the duodenum, it squeezes bile out to help break the fat into droplets. It supports digestion but does not directly handle blood sugar.

Spleen.name: Spleen
Spleen.function: Sits right beside the tail of the pancreas in the upper left abdomen. It filters the blood and recycles old red blood cells as part of the immune system. It is included here for orientation — it is the pancreas's close neighbour — rather than for a role in glucose control.
